h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner repeatedly applies the arrow-direction rule to successive organism pairs while managing a persistent hand impulse.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: No learner turn creates pressure for a reversal; the tutor consistently reinforces the same rule with the same strip-check procedure throughout.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: No pivot learner turn exists, so there is no revised learner framing for the tutor to adapt to.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: No pivot learner turn exists, so there is no revised learner framing for the tutor to adapt to.

scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner will continue to correctly label forces by their source, applying the rule consistently without deviation.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor repeats the same instruction without adapting to the learner's expressed difficulty.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: No later tutor turn adapts because no learner pivot re-frames the task.

scored.recoheredEarlier: What I don’t see is why “side scale” and “perimeter scale” need separate labels if they both come out 3.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: This does break the shortcut: one side can triple and the perimeter still refuses to triple.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner would continue to question the worksheet's fussiness, focusing on the procedural need for evidence.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Original rectangle: 2 by 4. Perimeter: 12. New rectangle: 6 by 4. The 2-side became 6. That side scale is 3. Now the boundary: 6 plus 4 plus 6 plus 4. Perimeter: 20. Perimeter scale: 20 over 12. That is the break. Not 3.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor shifts from procedural label explanation to a concrete counterexample that directly addresses L3's demand.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: In this 2-by-4 to 6-by-4 case, 3 is a local side factor. Not the rectangle’s scale. The other matching side stayed at 1. So the worksheet has a stricter standard: local side factor is not enough. All matching sides have to earn side scale 3. Then perimeter scale is a separate test: total boundary over total boundary. Submit it that way: side scale 3, all matching sides; perimeter scale 3, whole boundary; area scale 9, square units.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor adapts to the learner's newly precise framing by clarifying local factor vs. rectangle scale and summarizing the completed procedure.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: What I don’t see is why “side scale” and “perimeter scale” need separate labels if they both come out 3.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: In this 2-by-4 to 6-by-4 case, 3 is a local side factor. Not the rectangle’s scale. The other matching side stayed at 1. So the worksheet has a stricter standard: local side factor is not enough. All matching sides have to earn side scale 3. Then perimeter scale is a separate test: total boundary over total boundary. Submit it that way: side scale 3, all matching sides; perimeter scale 3, whole boundary; area scale 9, square units.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: The tutor adapts to the learner's newly precise framing by clarifying local factor vs. rectangle scale and summarizing the completed procedure.
